title: The Thought-Reader of Angels
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We hev tumbled ez dust
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or ez worms of the yearth;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wot we looked for hez bust!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We are objects of mirth!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ They have played us old Pards of the river! they hev played us for all we was worth!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was it euchre or draw
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Cut us off in our bloom?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was it faro, whose law
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is uncertain ez doom?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or an innocent ן¿½Jack potן¿½ that opened was to us ez the jaws of the tomb?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was nary! It kem
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With some sharps from the States.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ez folks sez, ן¿½All things kem
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To the fellers ez waits;ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And weן¿½d waited six months for that suthinן¿½ had me and Bill Nye in such straits!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And it kem. It was small;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was dream-like and weak;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It wore store clothes thatן¿½s all
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That we knew, so to speak;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But it called itself ן¿½Billson, Thought-Readerן¿½ which ainן¿½t half a name for its cheek!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He could read wot you thought,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he knew wot you did;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He could find things untaught,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No matter whar hid;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And he went to it, blindfold and smiling, being led by the hand like a kid!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then I glanced at Bill Nye,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And I sez, without pride,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Youן¿½ll excuse us. Weן¿½ve nigh
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On to nothinן¿½ to hide;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But if some gent will lend us a twenty, weן¿½ll hide it whar folks shall decide.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was Billsonן¿½s own self
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who forked over the gold,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With a smile. ן¿½Tharן¿½s the pelf,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He remarked. ן¿½I make bold
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To advance it, and go twenty better that Iן¿½ll find it without being told.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then I passed it to Nye,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Who repassed it to me.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And we bandaged each eye
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of that Billson ez we
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Softly dropped that coin in his coat pocket, ez the hull crowd around us could see.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That was all. Heן¿½d one hand
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Locked in mine. Then he groped.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We could not understand
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why that minit Nye sloped,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For we knew weן¿½d the dead thing on Billson even more than we dreamed of or hoped.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For he stood thar in doubt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With his hand to his head;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then he turned, and lit out
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Through the door where Nye fled,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dragginן¿½ me and the rest of us arter, while we larfed till we thought we was dead,

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till he overtook Nye
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And went through him. Words fail
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For what follers! Kin I
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Paint our agonized wail
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ez he drew from Nyeן¿½s pocket that twenty wot we sworn was in his own coat-tail!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And it was! But, when found,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It proved bogus and brass!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the question goes round
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How the thing kem to pass?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or, if passed, woz it passed thar by William; and I listens, and echoes ן¿½Alas!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For the days when the skill
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the keerds was no blind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When no effort of will
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Could beat four of a kind,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ When the thing wot you held in your hand, Pard, was worth more than the thing in your mind.ן¿½
meaning:
"The Thought-Reader of Angels" is a quintessential example of Bret Harte (Francis)'s signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
